## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define lyophilization. Why is it preferred over spray drying for protein pharmaceuticals?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Lyophilization is a low-temperature drying process in which water is removed from a frozen solution by **sublimation under high vacuum**. It is preferred over spray drying for proteins because (i) it operates at sub-ambient temperatures, preventing **thermal denaturation**; (ii) it removes water as ice without exposing the protein to liquid–vapour interfaces that cause aggregation; (iii) the resulting dry porous cake is highly stable, allowing **long shelf-life** at refrigerated temperatures; and (iv) it preserves the native three-dimensional conformation and bioactivity of the protein. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4 distinct reasons)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between primary drying and secondary drying in a lyophilization cycle.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Primary Drying | Secondary Drying |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | **Sublimation** of free ice | **Desorption** of bound water |
| Temperature | Shelf: −10 °C to +10 °C | Shelf: +30 °C to +40 °C |
| Pressure | 10 – 100 mTorr | Continued vacuum (lower) |
| Duration | 24 – 72 hours (longest) | 6 – 12 hours |
| Water removed | ~95 % of total | ~5 % (bound water) |
| Critical limit | Product T < Collapse Temp | Product T < Eutectic melt |

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the three stages of lyophilization with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of cryoprotectants in each stage.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] An enzyme formulation (5 mL in 10-mL vial) is to be freeze-dried. The vial inner cross-sectional area is $A = 3.46 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$. During primary drying, $T_{shelf} = -5\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{product} = -30\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $P_c = 13\ \text{Pa}$, $P_{ice}(-30\ ^\circ\text{C}) = 38\ \text{Pa}$, and $R_p = 6{,}000\ \text{m·s/kg}$. Given $\Delta H_{sub} = 2.84 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J/kg}$, compute the sublimation rate per vial in g/h and the heat transfer coefficient $K_v$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three stages (7 marks):**

1. **Freezing (1.5 marks):** Protein solution cooled to below its freezing point (~−1 °C to −5 °C) and further to −40 °C or below. Free water forms ice crystals; protein + cryoprotectant + bound water concentrate into an amorphous phase. Cooling must be **slow enough** to avoid excessive supercooling and small ice crystals that hinder drying, but **fast enough** to avoid cryoconcentration damage. Cryoprotectants like sucrose raise the **Tg'** and stabilize the protein against cold denaturation.

2. **Primary Drying (2.5 marks):** Chamber pressure reduced below triple point (~4.58 Torr). Shelf temperature raised (−10 °C to +10 °C). Ice sublimes; vapour travels through dried cake and condenses on the cold condenser. Heat flows through the partially dried layer to the sublimation front. Critical limit: **product temperature must remain below the collapse temperature (Tc)** to maintain cake structure. Cryoprotectants prevent collapse by raising Tc and maintaining amorphous stability.

3. **Secondary Drying (2 marks):** Shelf temperature raised to 30 °C – 40 °C under continued vacuum. Bound water is desorbed. Moisture drops to **< 1 %**. Cryoprotectants (especially trehalose) replace the hydrogen bonds previously made with water, stabilizing the protein in the dry state (water-replacement hypothesis).

4. **Diagram (1 mark):** Any clean schematic of the three sequential stages with parameters labelled.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the mass-transfer rate equation:**

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot (P_{ice} - P_c)}{R_p}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{(3.46 \times 10^{-4})\cdot(38 - 13)}{6{,}000}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{(3.46 \times 10^{-4})\cdot 25}{6{,}000} = \dfrac{8.65 \times 10^{-3}}{6{,}000}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = 1.4417 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to g/h:**

$$
\dfrac{dm}{dt} = 1.4417 \times 10^{-6}\times 3.6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{g/h} = 5.19\ \text{g/h per vial}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_v$:**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}\cdot(dm/dt)}{A\cdot(T_{shelf} - T_{product})}
$$

$$
K_v = \dfrac{(2.84 \times 10^{6})\cdot(1.4417 \times 10^{-6})}{(3.46 \times 10^{-4})\cdot(-5 - (-30))}
$$

$$
K_v = \dfrac{4.0944}{(3.46 \times 10^{-4})\cdot 25} = \dfrac{4.0944}{8.65 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
K_v \approx 473.3\ \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}
$$

**Final Answers:** Sublimation rate = **5.19 g/h per vial**; $K_v$ = **≈ 473 W/m²·K**.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the significance of the **glass transition temperature (Tg')** and **collapse temperature (Tc)** in designing a lyophilization cycle. How do cryoprotectant selection and shelf-temperature strategy depend on these temperatures?
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, calculate the vapour pressure of ice at **−40 °C** given that the vapour pressure of ice at **0 °C (273.15 K)** is **611.657 Pa** and the latent heat of sublimation $\Delta H_{sub} = 50{,}900\ \text{J/mol}$. State the engineering implication of your answer for lyophilizer operation at low product temperatures.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Significance of Tg' and Tc (7 marks):**

1. **Tg' (glass transition temperature of the frozen concentrate) (2 marks):** Below Tg', the amorphous matrix of protein + cryoprotectant is a rigid glass with negligible molecular mobility. Any process temperature must remain below Tg' during freezing and primary drying. Typical 5 % sucrose solution has Tg' ≈ **−32 °C**.

2. **Tc (collapse temperature) (1.5 marks):** This is the temperature above which the dry amorphous cake loses its porous structure and collapses. Tc is typically **2 °C – 5 °C above Tg'**. Collapse leads to: (i) loss of elegant cake appearance, (ii) increased reconstitution time, (iii) possible loss of bioactivity due to molecular mobility, (iv) batch rejection.

3. **Cryoprotectant selection strategy (2 marks):**
   - For formulations intended for low-temperature drying, choose protectants with **high Tg'** (e.g., sucrose > mannitol > glycine).
   - Trehalose gives the **highest Tg'** among disaccharides.
   - Protectant concentration ≥ 5 % w/v is recommended.

4. **Shelf-temperature strategy (1.5 marks):**
   - Operate with $T_{product} \le T_c - 2\ ^\circ\text{C}$ as the safety margin.
   - Adjust $T_{shelf}$ gradually in **ramped fashion** (not abrupt) to avoid overshoot.
   - Use thermocouples in select vials (edge vials run coldest, centre vials run hottest) to monitor real product temperature.

**(b) Clausius–Clapeyron numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sub}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** $P_0 = 611.657\ \text{Pa}$, $T_0 = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $T = 233.15\ \text{K}$ (−40 °C), $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$, $\Delta H_{sub} = 50{,}900\ \text{J/mol}$.

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{611.657}\right) = -\dfrac{50{,}900}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{233.15} - \dfrac{1}{273.15}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{233.15} = 4.2895 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{273.15} = 3.6610 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{233.15} - \dfrac{1}{273.15} = 6.285 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$
-\dfrac{50{,}900}{8.314} = -6{,}121.7\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$
-6{,}121.7 \times 6.285 \times 10^{-4} = -3.847
$$

**Step 6 — Take the exponential:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{611.657}\right) = -3.847
$$

$$
\dfrac{P}{611.657} = e^{-3.847} = 0.02136
$$

$$
P = 611.657 \times 0.02136 \approx 13.07\ \text{Pa} \approx 0.098\ \text{Torr}
$$

**Final Answer:** Vapour pressure of ice at −40 °C ≈ **13 Pa (≈ 0.098 Torr)**.

**Engineering Implication (carries over into the 7th mark):** This extremely low vapour pressure explains why lyophilization at −40 °C is **very slow** — the driving force $(P_{ice} - P_c)$ becomes small when both $P_{ice}$ is low and $P_c$ must be set even lower. Practical industrial lyophilization therefore operates with $T_{product}$ as **high as safely possible** (just below Tc) to maximize vapour pressure and hence sublimation rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tg' with Tc.** Tg' is the glass transition of the *frozen concentrate*; Tc is the *collapse* of the *dry cake*. They differ by 2 °C – 5 °C. Marks lost for using them interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Wrong units in mass-transfer equation.** $R_p$ must be in m·s/kg, $A$ in m², pressure in Pa. Mixing cm² and mm·s/kg leads to a numerical answer that is off by a factor of 10⁴.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert kg/s → g/h.** A common last-step error loses 1 mark per sub-question.
>
> 4. **Writing sublimation without heat/mass balance.** Examiners expect the *coupled* equation, not just one half.
>
> 5. **Calling lyophilization "evaporation".** It is **sublimation** (solid → vapour), not evaporation (liquid → vapour). Sublimation word is mandatory.
>
> 6. **Drawing the cycle wrong.** Freezing precedes primary drying, which precedes secondary drying. Reverse order = full mark deduction in diagram questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Lyophilization = freeze + sublimation + desorption** under vacuum.
- [ ] The **driving force** for primary drying is $(P_{ice}(T_p) - P_c)$.
- [ ] **Three stages in order:** Freezing → Primary Drying (sublimation) → Secondary Drying (desorption).
- [ ] **Glass transition temperature (Tg')** must be below the operating product temperature at all times.
- [ ] **Collapse temperature (Tc)** is typically **2 °C – 5 °C above Tg'**; exceeding it ruins the cake.
- [ ] **Operating window:** $T_c - 2\ ^\circ\text{C} \le T_{product} \le T_{shelf}$ and $P_c \approx 0.3 \cdot P_{ice}(T_{product})$.
- [ ] **Latent heat of sublimation of ice** ≈ **2,838 kJ/kg** = **2.84 × 10⁶ J/kg** = **50,900 J/mol**.
- [ ] **Chamber pressure** range: **10 – 100 mTorr** (1.3 – 13 Pa).
- [ ] **Condenser temperature:** **−50 °C to −85 °C**.
- [ ] **Final moisture content target:** **< 1 % w/w**.
- [ ] **Cryoprotectants** (sucrose, trehalose) raise Tg', prevent collapse, and replace water H-bonds.
- [ ] **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** governs $P_{ice}(T)$ and must be used to estimate driving force.
- [ ] **Heat transfer equation:** $Q = K_v \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{product}) = \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \dfrac{dm}{dt}$.
- [ ] **Mass transfer equation:** $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot (P_{ice} - P_c)}{R_p}$.
- [ ] **Coupled equation** equating the two defines the operating regime.
- [ ] Lyophilization is **NOT** evaporation — it is **sublimation** (write the word in every answer).
- [ ] **Yield calculation:** (mass of recovered dry powder / mass of initial solids) × 100.
- [ ] **Moisture by Karl Fischer or by oven drying** at 105 °C to constant weight.
- [ ] Lyophilized cakes are **porous, low-density, easily reconstituted** — never shiny, melted, or collapsed.
- [ ] **Applications:** mAbs, vaccines, enzymes, diagnostics, probiotic cultures, plasma, astronaut food.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** long cycle (24–72 h), high capital cost, high energy footprint.
- [ ] For a typical 5 mL vial, sublimation rate ≈ **3 – 8 g/h** is realistic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study on Crystallization of a Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBTL607 - DOWNSTREAM PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study on Crystallization of a Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Crystallization** is a mass-transfer, separation, and purification unit operation in which a dissolved solid product is converted from its soluble state (in a solvent) into an insoluble, ordered solid phase (crystals) through the deliberate manipulation of solution thermodynamics—typically by **cooling, evaporation, anti-solvent addition, or salting out**.

In the context of **Downstream Processing (DSP)**, crystallization is one of the final and most critical purification steps used in the manufacture of pharmaceuticals, fine chemicals, bioproducts, and food ingredients. It achieves simultaneous **purification, stabilization, and solid-form definition** in a single operation.

## 1.2 Key Governing Concepts (Syllabus Terminology)

| Term | Definition (KTU 2024 Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Supersaturation** | A non-equilibrium state where the solute concentration exceeds its equilibrium saturation value at a given temperature. |
| **Nucleation** | The birth of a new crystal phase (formation of a critical-sized cluster) from the supersaturated solution. |
| **Crystal Growth** | The addition of solute molecules/ions to an existing crystal face, increasing its dimensions. |
| **Mother Liquor** | The residual solution that remains in contact with the crystals after crystallization. It contains the impurities and the unrecovered product. |
| **Recrystallization** | Re-dissolving impure crystals in a fresh solvent and re-precipitating them to enhance purity. |
| **Yield** | The ratio of the actual mass of pure crystals recovered to the theoretical maximum mass, expressed as a percentage. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Honey Jar" Analogy** 🥃
> Imagine a jar of warm honey. Honey is essentially a highly concentrated sugar solution. As long as the honey is warm, the sugar remains dissolved and you see a clear, thick liquid. Now, place that jar in a refrigerator for several days. Tiny white sugar crystals begin to appear and slowly grow. **What happened?** By cooling, we *reduced the solubility* of sugar, forcing it to come out of solution as solid crystals. This is exactly what happens in industrial crystallization—the solvent (analogous to honey) is manipulated (cooled/evaporated) so the dissolved product (analogous to sugar) precipitates as a purified solid.

- **Solvent** = honey (the carrier fluid).
- **Solute (Product)** = sugar (what we want to recover).
- **Cooling** = refrigerator.
- **Crystals** = the white sugar grains formed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Crystallization does NOT just "trap" molecules—it selectively rejects impurities because only molecules with the correct geometry and size can fit into the growing crystal lattice. This is why crystallization produces very high-purity products, even though the mother liquor may still contain both product and impurities.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Crystallization Experiments

- **Saturation Concentration (C\*)** is measured in **grams of solute per 100 grams of solvent (g/100 g)** or **g/L**.
- **Supersaturation Ratio (S)** is dimensionless.
- **Yield** is reported in **% (percentage)**.
- **Purity** is reported in **% (w/w)** or by melting point comparison in **°C**.
- **Standard reference constants:** Melting point of pure compound (in °C) is used as the **purity benchmark**. The closer the experimental melting point to the literature value, the purer the product.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Pillar Logic of Crystallization

Every successful crystallization experiment, whether in a 250 mL beaker or a 10,000 L industrial crystallizer, follows three logical pillars:

### Pillar I — Supersaturation Generation (Creating the Driving Force)

Without supersaturation, no crystallization will occur. Supersaturation is the **driving force** for both nucleation and growth.

- **Method 1: Cooling Crystallization** — Reduces solubility by lowering temperature (best for solutes whose solubility drops sharply with temperature, e.g., potassium nitrate, benzoic acid).
- **Method 2: Evaporative Crystallization** — Removes solvent by boiling/evaporation, increasing the concentration of solute (best for solutes with weak temperature dependence, e.g., sodium chloride, sucrose).
- **Method 3: Anti-Solvent (Drowning-Out) Crystallization** — Addition of a miscible liquid in which the solute is insoluble (e.g., adding water to an alcoholic solution of a hydrophobic compound).
- **Method 4: Salting-Out** — Addition of a common ion or a highly soluble salt to decrease the solubility of the target product.

### Pillar II — Nucleation (Birth of Crystals)

- **Primary Nucleation** — Spontaneous formation of nuclei in the absence of crystals (driven by very high supersaturation).
- **Secondary Nucleation** — Formation of new nuclei due to the presence of existing crystals (gentle stirring/attrition). This is the preferred industrial mechanism because it is easier to control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does nucleation matter for the KTU lab?** If nucleation occurs too rapidly, you obtain many *small* crystals with poor filterability and high impurity entrainment. If nucleation is controlled (e.g., by slow cooling, seeding), you obtain *fewer but larger* crystals, which are easier to filter, wash, and dry.

### Pillar III — Crystal Growth (Size Enlargement)

Growth proceeds by diffusion of solute from bulk solution to the crystal surface, followed by integration into the lattice. The **growth rate** is described by:

$$ G = \frac{dL}{dt} = k_g \cdot (C - C^*)^g $$

where $L$ is the characteristic crystal size, $k_g$ is the growth rate constant, $C$ is the bulk solution concentration, $C^*$ is the saturation concentration, and $g$ is the growth order (typically 1 to 2).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every equation you may need** to answer numerical/or viva questions on the crystallization experiment in the KTU 2024 Scheme lab exam. No vertical bars (`|`) are used inside math expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / KTU Exam Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **% Yield** | $\dfrac{M_{\text{actual}}}{M_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Actual mass recovered ÷ theoretical maximum × 100 |
| 2 | **Theoretical Mass** | $M_{\text{theoretical}} = V \cdot C_{\text{sat}}$ | g | Volume of saturated solution × saturation concentration |
| 3 | **Mass Balance on Product** | $M_{\text{initial}} = M_{\text{crystals}} + M_{\text{mother liquor}}$ | g | Total accountability of the solute |
| 4 | **Supersaturation Ratio (S)** | $S = \dfrac{C}{C^*}$ | dimensionless | $S > 1$ implies supersaturation |
| 5 | **Degree of Supersaturation** | $\sigma = S - 1 = \dfrac{C - C^*}{C^*}$ | dimensionless | The actual driving force |
| 6 | **Crystal Growth Rate** | $G = k_g (C - C^*)^g$ | m/s | Empirical power-law growth |
| 7 | **Nucleation Rate** | $J = k_n (C - C^*)^n$ | nuclei/(volume·time) | Empirical power-law nucleation |
| 8 | **Recovery in Mother Liquor** | $R = \dfrac{C_f}{C_0} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of product left uncrystallized |
| 9 | **Purity Check (Melting Point)** | $\Delta T = T_{\text{lit}} - T_{\text{obs}}$ | °C | Smaller $\Delta T$ = higher purity |
| 10 | **Recrystallization Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{mass of pure crystal}}{\text{mass of crude input}} \times 100$ | % | Efficiency after re-crystallization |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Crystallization in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> Crystallization is one of the **highest-value unit operations** in chemical and pharmaceutical engineering because:
> - It simultaneously **purifies** and **formats** the product.
> - Crystals are easy to **filter, dry, package, and transport** (unlike liquid products).
> - It is energy-efficient compared to distillation for solid products.
> - It is used in **sugar refining, fertilizer production (urea, ammonium nitrate), pharmaceutical APIs (paracetamol, aspirin), bioproducts (amino acids, citric acid),** and **semiconductor-grade silicon purification.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of % Yield from First Principles (Mass Balance Approach)

This is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU viva and lab report assessments. We derive the yield expression rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the System

Consider a saturated solution at a chosen crystallization temperature $T_c$. The solvent volume is $V$ (in litres or mL), and the saturation solubility of the solute at $T_c$ is $C_{\text{sat}}(T_c)$ (in g/L or g/mL).

### Step 2 — Mass of Solute Initially Dissolved

The total mass of solute that was dissolved in the hot solution (just before cooling) is given by:

$$ M_{\text{initial}} = V \cdot C_{\text{hot}} $$

where $C_{\text{hot}}$ is the concentration in the hot, fully dissolved solution.

### Step 3 — Mass Remaining in Solution (Mother Liquor) After Cooling

After cooling to $T_c$, the residual concentration in the mother liquor is $C_{\text{sat}}(T_c)$. The mass still dissolved is:

$$ M_{\text{mother liquor}} = V \cdot C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) $$

### Step 4 — Theoretical Mass of Crystals

By conservation of mass, the maximum possible mass that can crystallize out is the difference between the initial dissolved mass and the mass remaining in the mother liquor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{theoretical}} &= M_{\text{initial}} - M_{\text{mother liquor}} \\
&= V \cdot C_{\text{hot}} - V \cdot C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \\
&= V \left( C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Define the Actual Mass Recovered

The student physically filters, washes, and weighs the crystals on a pre-weighed filter paper. This experimental mass is denoted $M_{\text{actual}}$ (in grams).

### Step 6 — The % Yield Equation

The percent yield is defined as the ratio of actual to theoretical mass, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Yield} &= \frac{M_{\text{actual}}}{M_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{M_{\text{actual}}}{V \left( C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \right)} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This expression is dimensionless when $M_{\text{actual}}$ and $M_{\text{theoretical}}$ share identical units (both in grams). Multiplying by 100 converts the decimal fraction into a percentage. The denominator is the *thermodynamic upper bound* of recovery; the actual yield is always **less than or equal to 100%** because of mechanical losses (filter paper retention, transfer loss, incomplete precipitation).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** 50 mL of a hot aqueous solution contains 12.0 g of benzoic acid fully dissolved at 90 °C. The solution is cooled to 20 °C, where the solubility of benzoic acid is 0.29 g/100 mL. The student recovers 5.05 g of dry benzoic acid crystals. Calculate (a) the theoretical yield, (b) the actual % yield, and (c) the mass of benzoic acid remaining in the mother liquor.

### Part (a) — Theoretical Yield

We use the standard formula, ensuring consistent units (convert 0.29 g/100 mL → 0.0029 g/mL):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{theoretical}} &= V \left( C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \right) \\
&= 50 \text{ mL} \times \left( \frac{12.0 \text{ g}}{50 \text{ mL}} - \frac{0.29 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ mL}} \right) \\
&= 50 \times (0.240 - 0.0029) \\
&= 50 \times 0.2371 \\
&= 11.855 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Actual % Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Yield} &= \frac{M_{\text{actual}}}{M_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5.05}{11.855} \times 100 \\
&\approx 42.6 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c) — Mass Remaining in Mother Liquor

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{mother liquor}} &= V \cdot C_{\text{sat}}(20\,^\circ\text{C}) \\
&= 50 \text{ mL} \times \frac{0.29 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ mL}} \\
&= 0.145 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Results

| Observation | Implication (Engineer's Diagnosis) |
|---|---|
| Yield = 42.6 % is low | Mechanical losses are large; filtration/transfer steps need optimization. |
| Most of the unrecovered mass is "missing" | Crystals may have been lost in transfer or mother liquor was not properly drained. |
| Mother liquor holds only 0.145 g | Theoretically, 11.855 g *should* have crystallized; the gap is operational, not thermodynamic. |
| If % Yield > 95% | Crystals were wet (still hold mother liquor) or mass includes impurities. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A yield of 100% is **suspicious** in practice. It almost always indicates that the crystals were not properly dried, that the mother liquor was incompletely drained, or that impurities have co-precipitated. KTU examiners expect you to recognize this paradox.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Mass Balance for a Recrystallization Step

When the impure crude solid is recrystallized, two distinct mass streams must be tracked:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{crude input}} &= M_{\text{pure crystals}} + M_{\text{mother liquor residue}} + M_{\text{impurity in mother liquor}} + M_{\text{mechanical loss}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **purification ratio** is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Purity} &= \frac{M_{\text{pure crystals}}}{M_{\text{crude input}} - M_{\text{mother liquor residue}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In a recrystallization, **yield and purity are inversely related.** If you evaporate the mother liquor to dryness to recover more mass, you also concentrate the impurities back into the solid, destroying the purification. This is the *crystallizer's dilemma* and is a frequent viva question in KTU lab assessments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Complete Lab Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Raw Product / Crude Solid"] --> stepB["Weigh Crude Sample (M1)"]
    stepB --> stepC["Dissolve in Minimum Volume of Hot Solvent"]
    stepC --> stepD["Hot Gravity Filtration to Remove Insoluble Impurities"]
    stepD --> stepE["Cool Filtrate Slowly to Room Temperature"]
    stepE --> stepF["Further Cool in Ice Bath to Maximize Recovery"]
    stepF --> stepG["Vacuum Buchner Funnel Filtration"]
    stepG --> stepH["Wash Crystals with Cold Solvent"]
    stepH --> stepI["Dry Crystals in Oven or Desiccator"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Weigh Dried Pure Crystals (M2)"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Measure Melting Point to Verify Purity"]
    stepK --> stepL["Calculate Percent Yield and Purity"]

    subgraph Analysis_and_Reporting
        stepL --> stepM["Compare with Literature Melting Point"]
        stepM --> stepN["Record Observations in Lab Record"]
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Cooling Crystallizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedStream
        F1["Saturated Hot Solution"] --> F2["Feed Inlet Valve"]
    end

    F2 --> F3["Jacketed Crystallization Vessel"]

    subgraph CrystallizerCore
        F3 --> F4["Internal Stirrer / Impeller"]
        F3 --> F5["Temperature Probe"]
        F3 --> F6["Cooling Jacket with Recirculating Chiller"]
    end

    F4 --> F7["Crystal Slurry Outlet"]
    F5 --> F7
    F6 --> F7

    F7 --> F8["Vacuum Filter / Buchner Funnel"]
    F8 --> F9["Wet Crystal Cake"]
    F9 --> F10["Drying Oven / Desiccator"]
    F10 --> F11["Final Dry Pure Crystals"]

    F8 --> F12["Mother Liquor Collection Flask"]
    F12 --> F13["Mother Liquor Reservoir for Recovery"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process State Diagram — Nucleation vs Growth Window

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Undersaturated
    Undersaturated --> Saturated : Add solute or cool
    Saturated --> Metastable : Mild supersaturation
    Metastable --> NucleationOnset : S exceeds critical value
    NucleationOnset --> GrowthDominant : Controlled cooling and seeding
    GrowthDominant --> CrystalHarvest : Target size reached
    CrystalHarvest --> [*]

    Undersaturated : No driving force
    Saturated : C equals C-star
    Metastable : Crystals grow, no new nuclei
    NucleationOnset : Burst of small crystals
    GrowthDominant : Few crystals enlarge steadily
```

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance of the Metastable Zone:** Skilled crystallizer operators maintain the solution in the **metastable zone** for as long as possible. This suppresses unwanted nucleation and favors growth, producing large, uniform, easy-to-filter crystals. Crossing the boundary into the nucleation zone produces a fine, chalky product that is difficult to handle. KTU lab viva questions often test this concept with diagrams similar to the state diagram above.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Define the term "Supersaturation" in the context of crystallization. How is it achieved experimentally?**

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Supersaturation is a non-equilibrium state of a solution in which the concentration of dissolved solute exceeds the equilibrium saturation concentration ($C > C^*$) at the given temperature, providing the thermodynamic driving force for crystallization.
- **Methods of achieving it (1.5 marks):** It can be achieved by (i) cooling the saturated solution, (ii) evaporating a portion of the solvent, (iii) adding an anti-solvent in which the solute is insoluble, or (iv) salting-out using a common-ion salt.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
**Differentiate between primary nucleation and secondary nucleation.**

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**

| Aspect | Primary Nucleation | Secondary Nucleation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Spontaneous formation of nuclei in a solution *without* pre-existing crystals. | Formation of new nuclei *induced* by the presence of parent crystals. |
| **Supersaturation Required** | Very high | Moderate |
| **Industrial Preference** | Avoided (gives fine, impure product) | Preferred (controlled, large crystals) |

(2 marks for definition of each + 1 mark for the comparative distinction.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step laboratory procedure** for the crystallization of benzoic acid from water as the solvent. Include the role of each step in achieving the desired purity and yield.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a lab experiment, a student dissolved **15.0 g of crude benzoic acid** in **75 mL of hot water at 95 °C**. The solution was cooled to 25 °C, at which the solubility of benzoic acid is **0.34 g / 100 mL**. The student recovered **9.20 g** of dry benzoic acid crystals. Calculate:
  (i) the **theoretical yield** of benzoic acid,
  (ii) the **% yield** obtained, and
  (iii) the **mass of benzoic acid remaining in the mother liquor**.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Weighing the crude sample** (1 mark): Accurately weigh 15.0 g of crude benzoic acid on an analytical balance and record as $M_1$.

2. **Dissolution in minimum hot solvent** (1 mark): Heat 75 mL of distilled water to ~95 °C in a beaker. Add the crude benzoic acid gradually with stirring until fully dissolved. The minimum solvent principle ensures maximum yield on cooling.

3. **Hot gravity filtration** (1 mark): Filter the hot solution through a pre-warmed fluted filter paper to remove insoluble impurities (dust, decolorizing carbon residue). Pre-warming prevents premature crystallization on the filter.

4. **Slow cooling to room temperature** (1 mark): Allow the filtrate to cool gradually to 25 °C. Slow cooling yields larger, more uniform crystals with higher purity. Sudden cooling produces a fine, impure precipitate.

5. **Ice-bath cooling** (1 mark): Further cool the solution in an ice bath for 15–30 minutes to maximize the recovery of dissolved product by exploiting the steep drop in solubility.

6. **Vacuum filtration** (1 mark): Collect the crystals on a pre-weighed Buchner funnel using vacuum. The mother liquor is removed into the filter flask.

7. **Washing and drying** (1 mark): Wash the crystal cake with a small volume of ice-cold water to remove adhering mother liquor (which holds the impurities). Dry in a desiccator or oven at ~50 °C until constant mass is achieved. The final dry mass is $M_2$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given data:** $M_{\text{crude}} = 15.0$ g; $V = 75$ mL; $C_{\text{hot}} = \dfrac{15.0}{75} = 0.200$ g/mL; $C_{\text{sat}}(25\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.34 \text{ g} / 100 \text{ mL} = 0.0034$ g/mL; $M_{\text{actual}} = 9.20$ g.

**(i) Theoretical yield (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{theoretical}} &= V \left( C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \right) \\
&= 75 \text{ mL} \times (0.200 - 0.0034) \text{ g/mL} \\
&= 75 \times 0.1966 \\
&= 14.745 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) % Yield (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Yield} &= \frac{M_{\text{actual}}}{M_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{9.20}{14.745} \times 100 \\
&\approx 62.4 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mass in mother liquor (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{mother liquor}} &= V \cdot C_{\text{sat}}(25\,^\circ\text{C}) \\
&= 75 \text{ mL} \times 0.0034 \text{ g/mL} \\
&= 0.255 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Verification of mass balance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{crystals}} + M_{\text{mother liquor}} + M_{\text{loss}} &= 15.0 \\
9.20 + 0.255 + M_{\text{loss}} &= 15.0 \\
M_{\text{loss}} &= 5.545 \text{ g} \quad (\text{unrecovered, mainly transfer losses})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B Alternate]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **theory of crystallization** with a neat labeled solubility curve. Discuss the **metastable zone** and its importance in producing good-quality crystals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical industry wishes to recover **aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid)** from 200 L of a saturated solution at 30 °C, where its solubility is 0.33 g / 100 mL. The solution is cooled to 5 °C, where solubility drops to 0.21 g / 100 mL. If the **actual recovery is 215 g**, calculate the **% yield** and comment on whether the recovery is acceptable for industrial practice.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Solubility Curve Description (2 marks):** A solubility curve plots solubility (g/100 g solvent) on the y-axis vs temperature (°C) on the x-axis. The curve typically rises for most solutes (e.g., benzoic acid, KNO₃) and is nearly flat for some (e.g., NaCl).

2. **The Three Zones (2 marks):**
   - **Stable (Undersaturated) zone** — below the solubility curve; no crystallization.
   - **Metastable zone** — narrow region above the curve; crystals can grow, but no new nuclei form spontaneously.
   - **Labile (Unstable) zone** — far above the curve; spontaneous nucleation occurs (burst of crystals).

3. **Industrial Strategy (1.5 marks):** Operate the crystallizer within the **metastable zone** to allow existing crystals to grow large while suppressing unwanted nucleation. This is achieved by slow cooling, controlled evaporation, or seeding.

4. **Significance of the Metastable Width (1.5 marks):** A wide metastable zone is industrially desirable because it gives a wider operating window for cooling/evaporation. Solutes with steep solubility curves (e.g., KNO₃) have wide metastable zones and are easy to crystallize by cooling.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $V = 200 \text{ L} = 200{,}000$ mL; $C_{\text{sat}}(30\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.33$ g/100 mL $= 0.0033$ g/mL; $C_{\text{sat}}(5\,^\circ\text{C}) = 0.21$ g/100 mL $= 0.0021$ g/mL; $M_{\text{actual}} = 215$ g.

**Theoretical yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{theoretical}} &= V \left( C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}}(T_c) \right) \\
&= 200{,}000 \text{ mL} \times (0.0033 - 0.0021) \text{ g/mL} \\
&= 200{,}000 \times 0.0012 \\
&= 240 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**% Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Yield} &= \frac{215}{240} \times 100 \\
&\approx 89.58 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Industrial Comment (2 marks):** A yield of ~89.6% is **acceptable** for a first-stage industrial crystallization of aspirin. Aspirin has a flat solubility curve, so cooling alone gives limited recovery. To improve yield, the industry uses **anti-solvent crystallization (water addition to an alcoholic solution)** or **evaporative crystallization** in combination. Aspirin is also typically recrystallized once or twice to meet pharmacopoeial purity (≥99.5% w/w).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in the Crystallization Lab Exam:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 to –3 marks):** Mixing g/100 mL with mL directly without conversion is the #1 mistake. Always convert solubility to g/mL or g/L first.
> 2. **Confusing "Yield" with "Purity" (–1 to –2 marks):** Yield is about *quantity recovered*; purity is about *how clean the recovered material is*. They are reported separately and use different formulae.
> 3. **Forgetting the Cold-Wash Step (–1 mark):** Failing to mention washing the crystals with ice-cold solvent is a frequent omission. The cold wash prevents re-dissolution of the product.
> 4. **Not Vacuum-Filtering Properly (–1 mark):** Using only gravity filtration on a Buchner funnel is incorrect; vacuum must be applied.
> 5. **Reporting 100% Yield (–1 mark):** This is physically impossible without extraordinary measures and signals to the examiner that you did not understand the concept.
> 6. **Missing the Purity Argument in Recrystallization (–2 marks):** Recrystallization sacrifices yield for purity. The student must explicitly state this trade-off.
> 7. **Not Drying Crystals to Constant Mass (–1 mark):** Wet crystals inflate the apparent yield and skew the mass balance.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

- Crystallization = **supersaturation + nucleation + growth**, in that logical order.
- The **supersaturation ratio** $S = C / C^*$ must be **greater than 1** for crystallization to begin.
- **Slow cooling + seeding** = large, pure, filterable crystals. Fast cooling = fine, impure, hard-to-filter crystals.
- The **solubility curve** is the master reference; it tells you which method (cooling vs. evaporative) is best.
- The **metastable zone** is the industrial operating sweet spot.
- **% Yield = (Actual mass ÷ Theoretical mass) × 100** — the single most important formula.
- **Theoretical mass = $V \times (C_{\text{hot}} - C_{\text{sat}})$** — derived from mass balance.
- **Purity is verified by melting point comparison**; pure compounds have sharp, narrow melting ranges.
- **Mother liquor** = the impure residual solution; it contains both unrecovered product and impurities.
- **Recrystallization** is used when the first crystals are not pure enough; it sacrifices yield for purity.
- **100% yield is suspicious** — it usually means wet crystals, incomplete drainage, or co-precipitation of impurities.
- **Minimum hot solvent principle:** Dissolve the product in the *least possible* hot solvent to maximize yield on cooling.
- **Filter while hot** to remove insoluble impurities; **wash with cold solvent** to remove soluble impurities without re-dissolving product.
- **Vacuum filtration** on a Buchner funnel is the standard separation technique in the KTU lab.
- **Constant mass drying** is mandatory before recording the final mass for yield calculation.
- **Key industrial applications:** sugar refining, urea/ammonium nitrate fertilizers, pharmaceutical APIs, citric acid, amino acids, semiconductor silicon.
- **Crystallization does not destroy product** (unlike distillation for thermally sensitive compounds), making it ideal for pharmaceuticals and bioproducts.
- **The trade-off** — **Yield ↑ ⟹ Purity ↓** and **Purity ↑ ⟹ Yield ↓**. This is the *crystallizer's dilemma* and a guaranteed viva question.
